seo.title: Pointers in C++ - Pointer Arithmetic, References, const Pointers, Dangling Pointers | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master C++ pointers including declaring pointers, address-of and dereference operators, nullptr, pointer arithmetic, pointers and arrays, pointer to pointer, references vs pointers, const pointers, void pointers, and dangling pointers. Includes 60+ practice questions with output prediction and coding challenges.
seo.keywords: c++ pointers, pointer arithmetic c++, references vs pointers c++, const pointer c++, dangling pointer c++, nullptr c++, pointer to pointer c++, void pointer c++, c++ pointer interview questions, c++ pointer examples

content.what:
What Are Pointers in C++?

A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Instead of holding a data value directly, a pointer holds the location in memory where that value resides. Pointers are one of the most powerful and distinctive features of C++ and are fundamental to understanding how the language manages memory.

Every variable you create occupies some bytes of memory at a specific address. A pointer lets you access and manipulate that memory directly. This is what makes C++ suitable for systems programming, embedded systems, and performance-critical applications.

Declaring Pointers

A pointer is declared by placing an asterisk (*) between the data type and the variable name:

int* p;       // p is a pointer to int
double* dp;   // dp is a pointer to double
char* cp;     // cp is a pointer to char

The * can be placed next to the type, next to the variable name, or in between. All three are valid: int* p, int *p, int * p. However, be careful with multiple declarations: int* a, b; declares a as a pointer and b as a regular int.

The Address-of (&) and Dereference (*) Operators

The address-of operator (&) returns the memory address of a variable. The dereference operator (*) accesses the value at the address stored in a pointer.

int x = 42;
int* p = &x;    // p stores the address of x
cout << p;      // prints the address (e.g., 0x7ffd5e8a)
cout << *p;     // prints 42 (the value at that address)
*p = 100;       // changes x to 100 through the pointer

nullptr (C++11)

A pointer that does not point to any valid memory should be set to nullptr. Before C++11, NULL or 0 was used, but nullptr is type-safe and preferred.

int* p = nullptr;  // p points to nothing
if (p == nullptr) {
    cout << "Pointer is null";
}
content.why:
Why Are Pointers Important?

1. Direct Memory Access

Pointers give you direct access to memory, which is essential for systems programming. Operating systems, device drivers, and embedded systems rely heavily on pointer-based memory manipulation. Understanding pointers means understanding how your program actually uses the hardware.

2. Efficient Function Arguments

Passing large objects (structs, arrays, strings) by value creates copies, which is slow. Passing a pointer (4 or 8 bytes regardless of object size) is much faster. This is why most C++ functions dealing with large data take pointers or references.

3. Dynamic Memory Allocation

The new operator returns a pointer to dynamically allocated memory on the heap. Without pointers, you cannot use dynamic memory at all. Data structures like linked lists, trees, and graphs are built entirely with pointers.

4. Arrays and Pointer Arithmetic

In C++, arrays and pointers are deeply connected. An array name decays to a pointer to its first element. Pointer arithmetic lets you traverse arrays without indexing, which is both powerful and efficient.

5. Interview and Placement Essential

Pointers are the most frequently tested C++ topic in campus placements and technical interviews at companies like Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Flipkart. Output prediction questions involving pointer arithmetic, pointer-to-pointer, and dangling pointers appear in almost every C++ interview.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Pointer Arithmetic

You can perform arithmetic on pointers. When you add 1 to a pointer, it moves forward by the size of the data type it points to (not by 1 byte).

int arr[] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int* p = arr;        // points to arr[0]
cout << *p;          // 10
cout << *(p + 1);    // 20 (moves 4 bytes forward for int)
cout << *(p + 3);    // 40
p++;                 // now p points to arr[1]
cout << *p;          // 20

If p is an int* and int is 4 bytes, then p + 1 adds 4 bytes, p + 2 adds 8 bytes, and so on. This is why pointer arithmetic is type-aware.

You can also subtract two pointers of the same type to find the number of elements between them: ptrdiff_t diff = p2 - p1;

2. Pointers and Arrays

An array name is essentially a constant pointer to the first element. When you pass an array to a function, it decays into a pointer.

int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
int* p = arr;           // arr decays to &arr[0]
cout << arr[1];         // 20
cout << *(arr + 1);     // 20 (same thing)
cout << p[1];           // 20 (pointer can use [] too)
cout << *(p + 1);       // 20

The expression arr[i] is internally equivalent to *(arr + i). This is why arrays and pointers are interchangeable in most contexts.

3. Pointer to Pointer (int**)

A pointer to a pointer stores the address of another pointer. This is commonly used for dynamic 2D arrays and for functions that need to modify a pointer itself.

int x = 10;
int* p = &x;
int** pp = &p;

cout << **pp;   // 10 (dereference twice: pp -> p -> x)
**pp = 50;      // changes x to 50

4. References vs Pointers

A reference is an alias for an existing variable. Unlike pointers, references cannot be null, cannot be reassigned to refer to a different variable, and do not require dereferencing.

int x = 10;
int& ref = x;    // ref is an alias for x
ref = 20;        // x is now 20 (no * needed)
cout << x;       // 20

When to use which:

Use references when: you always need a valid target (no null), you want cleaner syntax, you are passing to functions for modification (pass by reference).

Use pointers when: the target might be null, you need to reassign to different targets, you need pointer arithmetic, you are working with dynamic memory (new/delete).

5. const Pointers

There are three levels of const with pointers:

Pointer to const: const int* p -- you cannot modify the value through the pointer, but you can change what the pointer points to.

const int* p = &x;
// *p = 20;  // ERROR: cannot modify value
p = &y;      // OK: can change what p points to

const pointer: int* const p -- you can modify the value, but the pointer itself cannot point to something else.

int* const p = &x;
*p = 20;      // OK: can modify value
// p = &y;   // ERROR: cannot change pointer

const pointer to const: const int* const p -- neither the value nor the pointer can change.

const int* const p = &x;
// *p = 20;  // ERROR
// p = &y;   // ERROR

6. Passing Pointers to Functions

Passing a pointer to a function allows the function to modify the original variable (simulating pass-by-reference):

void increment(int* p) {
    (*p)++;    // modifies the original variable
}

int x = 10;
increment(&x);
cout << x;     // 11

7. Returning Pointers from Functions

A function can return a pointer, but you must never return a pointer to a local variable (it becomes a dangling pointer after the function returns):

// WRONG: returns pointer to local variable
int* bad() {
    int x = 10;
    return &x;  // x is destroyed when function returns
}

// CORRECT: return pointer to dynamically allocated memory
int* good() {
    int* p = new int(10);
    return p;   // caller must delete this
}

8. Dangling Pointers

A dangling pointer points to memory that has been freed or has gone out of scope. Accessing a dangling pointer is undefined behavior.

int* p = new int(10);
delete p;       // memory freed
// cout << *p;  // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR: dangling pointer
p = nullptr;    // good practice: set to nullptr after delete

9. Wild Pointers

A wild pointer is a pointer that has not been initialized. It contains a garbage address.

int* p;          // wild pointer: points to random address
// cout << *p;   // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOR
int* q = nullptr; // safe: initialized to nullptr

10. Void Pointers

A void* is a generic pointer that can point to any data type. You cannot dereference a void pointer directly -- you must cast it first.

int x = 10;
void* vp = &x;
// cout << *vp;          // ERROR: cannot dereference void*
cout << *(int*)vp;       // OK: cast to int* first, prints 10

Void pointers are used in C-style APIs and generic data structures. In modern C++, templates and std::any are preferred alternatives.

codeExamples.title: Basic Pointer Operations -- Declare, Assign, Dereference
codeExamples.explanation: p stores the address of x. *p dereferences the pointer to get the value 42. Assigning *p = 100 modifies x through the pointer. Reassigning p = &y makes the pointer point to a different variable.
codeExamples.output:
Value of x: 42
Address of x: 0x7ffd... (varies)
Value of p (address): 0x7ffd... (same as above)
Value at *p: 42
After *p = 100, x = 100
After p = &y, *p = 200

codeExamples.title: Pointer Arithmetic with Arrays
codeExamples.explanation: The array name arr decays to a pointer to the first element. *(p + i) accesses the i-th element. q++ advances the pointer by sizeof(int) bytes (typically 4). Pointer subtraction gives the number of elements between two addresses, not the byte difference.
codeExamples.output:
Using pointer arithmetic:
*(p + 0) = 10
*(p + 1) = 20
*(p + 2) = 30
*(p + 3) = 40
*(p + 4) = 50

Using pointer increment:
*q = 10
*q = 20
*q = 30
*q = 40
*q = 50

Elements between start and end: 4

codeExamples.title: Pointer to Pointer (int**)
codeExamples.explanation: pp is a pointer to a pointer. It stores the address of p, which in turn stores the address of x. **pp dereferences twice: first to get p, then to get x. Modifying **pp changes the value of x.
codeExamples.output:
x = 10
*p = 10
**pp = 10

Address of x: 0x7ffd... (varies)
p (holds address of x): 0x7ffd...
Address of p: 0x7ffd...
pp (holds address of p): 0x7ffd...

After **pp = 50:
x = 50
*p = 50
**pp = 50

codeExamples.title: References vs Pointers
codeExamples.explanation: Both functions swap two integers, but the pointer version requires & at the call site and * inside the function, while the reference version has cleaner syntax. A reference (ref) is an alias for val -- modifying ref directly modifies val without dereferencing.
codeExamples.output:
Before pointer swap: x=10, y=20
After pointer swap: x=20, y=10

Before reference swap: x=10, y=20
After reference swap: x=20, y=10

val after ref = 200: 200

codeExamples.title: const Pointers -- Three Levels of const
codeExamples.explanation: const int* p1 -- the const is on the left of *, so the value is const (read-only through the pointer). int* const p2 -- the const is on the right of *, so the pointer itself is const. const int* const p3 -- both are const. Read it right-to-left: p3 is a const pointer to a const int.
codeExamples.output:
p1 points to: 20
a after *p2 = 30: 30
p3 points to: 30

codeExamples.title: Passing and Returning Pointers -- Functions
codeExamples.explanation: doubleValue takes a pointer and modifies the original variable. fillArray receives an array as a pointer and fills it using pointer arithmetic. createArray allocates memory with new and returns the pointer -- the caller is responsible for calling delete[] to avoid a memory leak.
codeExamples.output:
After doubleValue: x = 50
Filled array: 100 101 102 103 104
Dynamic array: 10 20 30 40

codeExamples.title: Dangling Pointers, Wild Pointers, and Void Pointers
codeExamples.explanation: After delete p, the pointer becomes dangling -- it holds an address that is no longer valid. Setting it to nullptr after delete prevents accidental access. An uninitialized pointer (wild pointer) holds a garbage address. A void* can point to any type but must be cast to the correct type before dereferencing.
codeExamples.output:
Before delete: *p = 42
After delete: p is null
safe is null

void* pointing to int: 100
void* pointing to double: 3.14

commonMistakes.title: Dereferencing a Null Pointer
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
int* p = nullptr;
cout << *p;   // CRASH: segmentation fault
commonMistakes.error: Segmentation fault (runtime crash). Accessing memory at address 0 is undefined behavior.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always check if a pointer is nullptr before dereferencing. Dereferencing a null pointer causes undefined behavior, typically a segmentation fault that crashes the program.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing int* a, b (Only a is a Pointer)
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
int* a, b;    // a is int*, b is just int
b = &a;       // ERROR: b is not a pointer
commonMistakes.error: error: cannot convert 'int**' to 'int' in assignment
commonMistakes.explanation: In int* a, b;, the * binds to a, not to the type. Only a is a pointer; b is a regular int. To declare multiple pointers, place * before each variable name, or declare them on separate lines.

commonMistakes.title: Returning Pointer to Local Variable (Dangling Pointer)
commonMistakes.error: warning: address of local variable 'x' returned. Accessing *p is undefined behavior.
commonMistakes.explanation: Local variables are allocated on the stack and destroyed when the function returns. Returning their address creates a dangling pointer. Use new to allocate on the heap, or return by value instead.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting to Delete Dynamically Allocated Memory
commonMistakes.error: No compilation error, but memory is leaked every time process() is called. The program's memory usage grows indefinitely.
commonMistakes.explanation: Every new must have a matching delete, and every new[] must have a matching delete[]. Forgetting to delete causes a memory leak. In modern C++, prefer smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) to avoid manual memory management.

commonMistakes.title: Using delete Instead of delete[] for Arrays
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
int* arr = new int[10];
delete arr;    // WRONG: should be delete[]
commonMistakes.error: Undefined behavior. Only the first element's destructor is called. For primitive types it may appear to work, but for class types it corrupts memory.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
int* arr = new int[10];
delete[] arr;  // CORRECT: frees the entire array
commonMistakes.explanation: Memory allocated with new[] must be freed with delete[]. Using plain delete on an array is undefined behavior because the runtime does not know how many elements to destroy.

summary: A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Declare with int* p; and assign with p = &x;
The address-of operator (&) gets a variable's address. The dereference operator (*) accesses the value at the stored address.
nullptr (C++11) is the type-safe null pointer constant. Always initialize pointers to nullptr if they do not point to valid memory.
Pointer arithmetic is type-aware: p + 1 moves forward by sizeof(*p) bytes. For int* on a 4-byte system, p + 1 advances 4 bytes.
Array names decay to pointers. arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr + i). Pointers can use array indexing syntax: p[i].
A pointer to pointer (int** pp) stores the address of another pointer. Dereference twice (**pp) to reach the final value.
References (int& ref = x) are aliases that cannot be null or reassigned. Prefer references for function parameters; use pointers when null is possible or reassignment is needed.
const int* p means the value is read-only. int* const p means the pointer cannot be reassigned. const int* const p means both are fixed.
Dangling pointers point to freed or out-of-scope memory. Wild pointers are uninitialized. Both cause undefined behavior when dereferenced.
Void pointers (void*) can point to any type but must be cast before dereferencing. Modern C++ prefers templates over void pointers.

topicSpecific.hint: The dereference operator (*) accesses the value at the address stored in the pointer.
topicSpecific.explanation: p stores the address of x. *p dereferences the pointer to get the value at that address, which is 10.

topicSpecific.hint: Modifying *p modifies the variable that p points to.
topicSpecific.explanation: *p = 15 writes 15 to the memory location that p points to, which is a. So a becomes 15.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
cout << *arr;
topicSpecific.hint: An array name decays to a pointer to its first element.
topicSpecific.explanation: arr decays to a pointer to the first element (&arr[0]). Dereferencing it with *arr gives the value of the first element, which is 10.

topicSpecific.hint: A reference is an alias for the original variable.
topicSpecific.explanation: ref is an alias for x. Assigning ref = 200 directly modifies x. No dereferencing is needed with references.

topicSpecific.hint: nullptr means the pointer does not point to valid memory.
topicSpecific.explanation: p is initialized to nullptr. The condition p == nullptr is true, so "null" is printed. The else branch (which would crash) is not executed.

topicSpecific.hint: p += 2 advances the pointer by 2 elements. p[-1] is equivalent to *(p - 1).
topicSpecific.explanation: p += 2 moves the pointer to arr[2] (value 30). *(p + 1) accesses arr[3] (40). p[-1] is *(p - 1) which accesses arr[1] (20).

topicSpecific.hint: A pointer to pointer requires double dereferencing to reach the final value.
topicSpecific.explanation: **pp dereferences twice: pp -> p -> x, giving 5. **pp = 25 writes 25 to x through two levels of indirection. So x becomes 25.

topicSpecific.hint: The function receives a pointer to x and modifies the value at that address.
topicSpecific.explanation: The function receives the address of x. Inside the function, *p = *p + 10 reads the value (5), adds 10, and writes 15 back. Since the function modifies the original memory, x is now 15.

topicSpecific.hint: Pointer subtraction gives the number of elements between two addresses.
topicSpecific.explanation: arr + 4 points to arr[4] (value 5). p - arr computes the number of elements between the two pointers, which is 4 (not the byte difference).

topicSpecific.hint: p points to arr[0], q points to arr[3]. Compute each expression carefully.
topicSpecific.explanation: *p is 10, *q is 40, sum is 50. *(p+1) is 20, *(q-1) is 30, sum is 50. q - p is the number of elements between them: 3.

topicSpecific.hint: The pointer traverses the string character by character until the null terminator.
topicSpecific.explanation: The loop prints each character of "Hello" by advancing the pointer. After the loop, p points to the null terminator at index 5. p - str gives 5 (the length of the string).

topicSpecific.hint: const int* means the value is read-only through the pointer, but the pointer can be reassigned.
topicSpecific.answer:
10
20
(The commented line would NOT compile: cannot modify value through pointer to const)
topicSpecific.explanation: const int* p means you cannot modify the value through p. But you can change what p points to. So p = &y is valid, but *p = 30 would fail.

topicSpecific.hint: *p++ dereferences first then increments the pointer. *++p increments the pointer first then dereferences. ++*p increments the value.
topicSpecific.explanation: *p++: post-increment -- dereferences p (gets 5), then advances p to arr[1]. *p: p is now at arr[1], so 10. *++p: pre-increment -- advances p to arr[2] first, then dereferences (15). ++*p: dereferences p (arr[2] = 15), then increments the value to 16.

mixed.hint: Dereference both pointers and add the values.
mixed.explanation: *p gives 3 (value of a), *q gives 7 (value of b). Their sum is 10.

mixed.hint: Both the pointer and the reference point to the same variable x.
mixed.explanation: Both p and ref refer to x. *p = 20 changes x to 20. Since ref is an alias for x, it reads the updated value 20.

mixed.question: What is the difference between a null pointer and a wild pointer?
mixed.hint: One is intentionally set to nothing; the other was never set at all.
mixed.answer: A null pointer is explicitly set to nullptr -- it points to nothing and can be safely checked. A wild pointer is an uninitialized pointer that contains a garbage address. Dereferencing either is undefined behavior, but a null pointer can be checked (if (p != nullptr)), while a wild pointer gives no indication that it is invalid.
mixed.explanation: Always initialize pointers. If you do not have a valid address yet, set the pointer to nullptr. An uninitialized pointer may contain any address, and dereferencing it can corrupt memory or crash the program unpredictably.

mixed.hint: *(p + i) accesses each element of the array through the pointer.
The function receives a pointer to pointer and changes what the original pointer points to.
mixed.explanation: Initially p points to a (10). changePointer receives &p and changes *pp (which is p) to &b. After the call, p points to b (20).

mixed.question: When should you use a pointer instead of a reference in C++?
mixed.hint: Think about null values, reassignment, and dynamic memory.
mixed.answer: Use pointers when: (1) the target might be null/optional, (2) you need to reassign to different objects during the lifetime, (3) you need pointer arithmetic, (4) you are working with new/delete (dynamic memory). Use references when: (1) the target is always valid, (2) you want cleaner syntax without * and &, (3) you are passing function parameters for modification.
mixed.explanation: References are safer (cannot be null, cannot dangle as easily) and cleaner (no dereferencing syntax). Pointers are more flexible (nullable, reassignable, support arithmetic). In modern C++, prefer references for function parameters and use smart pointers instead of raw pointers for heap memory.

mixed.hint: Three levels of indirection. Dereference three times to reach x.
mixed.explanation: ***ppp dereferences three times: ppp -> pp -> p -> x = 5. ***ppp = 99 writes 99 to x through three levels. So x becomes 99.

mixed.hint: arr is an array of pointers. p is a pointer to the first element of that array.
mixed.explanation: arr is an array of int*. p points to arr[0]. **p = *arr[0] = *(&a) = 10. After p++, p points to arr[1]. **p = *(&b) = 20. **(p+1) = *arr[2] = *(&c) = 30.

mixed.hint: Both a and &b refer to the original variables. Trace the operations step by step.
mixed.explanation: Inside mystery: *a is x and b is y. *a += b: x = 3 + 4 = 7. b += *a: y = 4 + 7 = 11. After the call: x = 7, y = 11.

mcqs.question: What does the & operator do when used with a variable?

options.A: Multiplies the variable by its address
options.B: Returns the memory address of the variable

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The & operator, when used as a unary operator with a variable (e.g., &x), returns its memory address. In declarations like int& ref = x, it creates a reference (D), but the question asks about using it with an existing variable.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to declare a pointer to an integer in C++?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. int *p; (or int* p;) declares a pointer to an integer. int p; (A) declares a regular integer. pointer int p; (C) is not valid C++ syntax. int &p; (D) declares a reference, which must be initialized.

mcqs.question: What value should an uninitialized pointer be set to in modern C++?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. nullptr (C++11) is the type-safe null pointer constant. NULL (B) and 0 (A) work but are not type-safe -- they can be confused with integers. void (D) is a type, not a value.

mcqs.question: What is the output of sizeof(int*) on a 64-bit system?

options.C: Depends on the type pointed to

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. On a 64-bit system, all pointers are 8 bytes because they must be able to address the entire 64-bit address space. The size of a pointer does not depend on the type it points to -- int*, double*, and char* are all 8 bytes on 64-bit.

mcqs.question: Which statement about references in C++ is true?

options.B: A reference can be reassigned to another variable after initialization
options.C: A reference must be initialized when declared
options.D: A reference requires dereferencing with * to access the value

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A reference must be initialized at declaration and cannot be null (A is false) or reassigned to another variable (B is false). References are accessed directly without any dereference operator (D is false).

mcqs.question: What does the expression arr[i] internally translate to in C++?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Array subscript arr[i] is syntactic sugar for *(arr + i). The array name decays to a pointer to the first element, pointer arithmetic computes the address of the i-th element, and the dereference operator accesses the value.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between const int* p and int* const p?

options.B: const int* p prevents modifying the value; int* const p prevents changing the pointer
options.C: const int* p prevents changing the pointer; int* const p prevents modifying the value
options.D: Both prevent modifying the value and the pointer

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. const int* p: the value pointed to is const (cannot do *p = ...). int* const p: the pointer itself is const (cannot do p = ...). Read right-to-left: 'p is a const pointer to int' vs 'p is a pointer to const int'.

mcqs.question: What happens when you increment a pointer of type double*?

options.D: The address increases by the value it points to

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Pointer arithmetic is type-aware. Incrementing a double* advances the address by sizeof(double), which is 8 bytes. This ensures the pointer moves to the next element in a double array.

mcqs.question: What is a dangling pointer?

options.B: A pointer that points to freed or out-of-scope memory
options.C: A pointer that has never been initialized
options.D: A pointer to a const variable

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A dangling pointer points to memory that has been deallocated (via delete) or has gone out of scope (local variable of a returned function). A null pointer (A) is safe to check. An uninitialized pointer (C) is called a wild pointer. A pointer to const (D) is perfectly valid.

mcqs.question:
What is the output?
int arr[] = {1, 2, 3};
cout << 2[arr];
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. 2[arr] is valid C++. Since arr[i] is *(arr + i), and addition is commutative, 2[arr] is *(2 + arr) = *(arr + 2) = arr[2] = 3.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT possible with references in C++?

options.B: Passing to a function by reference
options.C: Returning a reference from a function
options.D: Using a reference as a class member

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. C++ does not allow arrays of references because references are not objects -- they do not have their own address or size. You can pass by reference (B), return references (C), and use reference members in classes (D, but they must be initialized in the constructor initializer list).

mcqs.question: What is the result of subtracting two pointers of the same type?

options.A: The byte difference between the addresses
options.B: The number of elements between the two addresses

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Subtracting two pointers of the same type yields a ptrdiff_t value representing the number of elements between them, not the byte difference. For int* where int is 4 bytes, if p2 - p1 = 3, the byte difference is 12 but the result is 3.

mcqs.question:
What is wrong with this code?
void* vp = new int(10);
cout << *vp;

options.A: void* cannot hold the address of an int
options.C: void* cannot be dereferenced without casting

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A void* can hold any address (A is false), and new int(10) is valid (B is false). However, you cannot dereference a void* because the compiler does not know the size of the data. You must cast it first: *(static_cast(vp)).

mcqs.question: What happens if you use delete instead of delete[] on an array allocated with new[]?

options.A: Only the first element is freed

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Using delete on memory allocated with new[] is undefined behavior. The runtime may not call destructors for all elements and may corrupt internal bookkeeping. Always match new with delete and new[] with delete[].

codingChallenges.title: Reverse Array Using Pointers
codingChallenges.description: Write a function reverseArray(int* arr, int size) that reverses an array in-place using pointer arithmetic (no indexing with []). Print the array before and after reversing.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use only pointer arithmetic and dereferencing. Do not use array subscript operator [].

codingChallenges.title: String Length Using Pointers
codingChallenges.description: Write a function int strLen(const char* str) that returns the length of a C-string using pointer arithmetic. Do not use strlen().
codingChallenges.constraints: Use a pointer to traverse until the null terminator. Do not use any library function.

codingChallenges.title: Swap Two Pointers
codingChallenges.description: Write a function that takes two int** parameters and swaps what the original pointers point to. After the swap, the first pointer should point to the second variable and vice versa.
codingChallenges.constraints: The function must modify the original pointers using pointer-to-pointer. The values of a and b must NOT change.

codingChallenges.title: Find Min and Max Using Pointers
codingChallenges.description: Write a function findMinMax(int* arr, int size, int* min, int* max) that finds the minimum and maximum values in an array and stores them through pointers. The function should not return anything.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use pointer parameters for output. Traverse the array using pointer arithmetic.

codingChallenges.title: Dynamic Array -- Create, Fill, and Print
codingChallenges.description: Write a program that asks the user for a size n, dynamically allocates an array of n integers, fills it with the first n squares (1, 4, 9, ...), prints the array using pointer arithmetic, and properly deallocates the memory.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use new[] to allocate, pointer arithmetic to fill and print, and delete[] to free. Do not use vector.

codingChallenges.title: Matrix Transpose Using Dynamic 2D Array
codingChallenges.description: Dynamically allocate a 3x3 matrix using an array of pointers (int**), fill it with values 1-9, transpose it, and print both the original and transposed matrices. Free all allocated memory.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use int** for the 2D array. Allocate rows with new int*[rows] and each row with new int[cols]. Free in reverse order.
